title: AI: It’s Usage in North American Companies
text:
About Authors
Welcome to our website on AI adoption among U.S. firms. We are three Economics majors at Macalester College with a shared interest in how data and technology shape economies.
David Rios served as the technical lead for this project, handling API data extraction, data pipeline construction, and overall code architecture.
Katherine Manuel served as the primary point of contact and project coordinator, keeping the team on track across milestones and deadlines.
Isauro Reyes served as the style lead, shaping the visual presentation and overall communication of our findings.
crumbs: 1  AI Adoption in U.S. Firms

title: AI: It’s Usage in North American Companies
text:
Motivation
With how much companies are adopting artificial intelligence into its services, it seems like nowadays it’s impossible to escape from it. This is especially noticeable with tech companies, to the point where AI has become necessity to include in an expanding tech landscape. It’s almost hard to imagine that life wasn’t always like this. Not 20 years, 15, nor even 10 years ago.
This is precisely why we chose this topic for our final project.
crumbs: 1  AI Adoption in U.S. Firms

title: AI: It’s Usage in North American Companies
text:
Research Question

What are the AI adoption trends across industries and firms in the U.S.?
crumbs: 1  AI Adoption in U.S. Firms

title: AI: It’s Usage in North American Companies
text:
Background

@Someone please fill this section by reading a paper or smth as background and seed paper for our project.
crumbs: 1  AI Adoption in U.S. Firms

title: AI: It’s Usage in North American Companies
text:
Data
This project analyzes two datasets from the U.S. Census Bureau’s Annual Business Survey (ABS), accessed through the Census Data API.
The first is the ABSTCB (Annual Business Survey: Technology Characteristics of Businesses, 2018), which captures how U.S. employer firms used, produced, and were impacted by various technologies — including artificial intelligence, cloud computing, robotics, specialized software, and specialized equipment. This is the only year for which this technology module is publicly available, making 2018 our reference point for AI adoption patterns.
The second is the ABSCS (Annual Business Survey: Company Summary, 2017–2021), which provides firm-level aggregates including number of firms, employment, annual payroll, and revenue across industries and states over time. This panel dataset allows us to track how industries evolved before and after the 2018 AI snapshot.
Data Collection
Both datasets are collected by the U.S. Census Bureau as part of the Annual Business Survey, conducted annually since 2018. The ABS covers all nonfarm employer businesses in the United States and is designed to provide nationally representative statistics on business characteristics, ownership demographics, innovation, and technology use. Firms are sampled and weighted to represent the broader population of U.S. employer businesses.
Data Acquisition
Both datasets were accessed programmatically through the Census Data API using R’s httr2 package. We constructed a reusable API fetcher function that retrieves data by year, module, and geography. The technology data was pulled at the state level across all 50 states plus Washington D.C., and the company summary panel was pulled for each year from 2017 to 2021.
Data Understanding

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Motivations
In this document, I will be looking at the evolution of AI. Since we only have data for 2018 on AI-usage, I would look within those industries leading the AI race, how much have their payroll evolve. We excluded the total observations, as well as the unknown category.

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Introduction

Codelibrary(here)
library(tidyverse)
library(tigris)
library(stringr)
library(scales)
library(ggrepel)
library(tidymodels)
library(ranger)
library(patchwork)
library(leaflet) 
library(forcats)
library(rpart)
library(tibble)   
library(rpart.plot)


source(here::here("data", "data_cleaning.R"))

## Theme Settings
theme_set(
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + 
    theme(
      legend.position = "right",
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
      plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 10),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()))

title: David’s Exploration
section: Who’s the top industry using AI?
text:
Who’s the top industry using AI?
I am interested in seeing who was the top industry in the “AI race” in 2018.

Codeai_industry_leaders <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence", 
    !is.na(used_tech), 
    used_tech != "Unknown") |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  summarise(
    total_firms = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    ai_firms = sum(n_firms[used_tech == "Used"], na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(ai_rate = ai_firms / total_firms) |>
  filter(total_firms > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(ai_rate))

ai_industry_leaders |> 
  filter(ai_firms>0) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(naics_label, ai_rate), y = ai_rate)) +
  geom_col() + 
  scale_fill_viridis_b()+
  coord_flip() + 
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Which Industries Led the AI Race in 2018?",
    subtitle = "Percentage of firms reporting AI Use",
    x = NULL,
    y = "AI Adoption Rate",
    caption = "Management took the lead in 2018, with an AI implementation of 7.7%. \nSource: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS) | Author: David Rios ") +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), 
        axis.ticks.y = element_blank())

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Payroll Level Change
I then got interested in analyzing what’s the payroll evolution by industry.

Codetech_2018_summary <- tech_2018_clean |> 
  group_by(year, used_tech, use_level) |> 
  summarise(
    total_firms   = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_emp     = sum(n_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_payroll = sum(payroll, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop")

payroll_all <- cs_panel_clean |>
  group_by(year, naics_label) |>
  summarise(total_payroll = sum(payroll * 1000, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

top_4_industries <- ai_industry_leaders |> slice_max(ai_rate, n = 4) |> pull(naics_label)
payroll_top4 <- payroll_all |> filter(naics_label %in% top_4_industries)

ggplot() +
  geom_line(data = payroll_all, 
            aes(x = year, y = total_payroll, group = naics_label), 
            color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_line(data = payroll_top4, 
            aes(x = year, y = total_payroll, color = naics_label, group = naics_label), 
            linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = payroll_top4, 
             aes(x = year, y = total_payroll, color = naics_label), 
             size = 1.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar(scale = 1e-9, suffix = " B", accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "H") +
  labs(
    title = "Payroll Evolution",
    subtitle = "Highlighting the 4 industries with the highest AI adoption rates in 2018",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Annual Payroll (Billions)",
    color = NULL,
    caption = "Healthcare is the top industry by total payroll, despite showing low AI adoption rates in 2018.  \nSource: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS) | Author: David Rios "
  ) + theme(legend.position = "top")

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Payroll Percentage Change
Payroll percentage change would be a better viz, and I took 2017 as the base year.

Code## indexed payroll growth
payroll_growth <- payroll_all |>
  arrange(naics_label, year) |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  mutate(
    payroll_growth_pct = total_payroll / first(total_payroll)) |> #base year: 2017
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(naics_label = str_wrap(naics_label, width = 35))


## Top AI industries
top_4_industries <- ai_industry_leaders |>
  slice_max(ai_rate, n = 4) |>
  pull(naics_label)

payroll_growth_top4 <- payroll_growth |>
  filter(naics_label %in% top_4_industries)


## Bottom AI industries
w_4_industries <- ai_industry_leaders |>
  slice_min(ai_rate, n = 4) |>
  pull(naics_label)

payroll_growth_w4 <- payroll_growth |>
  filter(naics_label %in% w_4_industries)


# plots
p1_top4_ai <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = payroll_growth,
    aes(x = year, y = payroll_growth_pct, group = naics_label),
    color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_line(
    data = payroll_growth_top4,
    aes(x = year, y = payroll_growth_pct, color = naics_label),
    linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = payroll_growth_top4 |> filter(year == max(year)),
    aes(x = year, y = payroll_growth_pct, label = naics_label, color = naics_label),
    nudge_x = 1,
    show.legend = FALSE,
    size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  labs(
    title = "AI-Leading Industries",
    subtitle = "Indexed to 2017 total payroll",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Payroll (2017 = 1)") +
  theme_minimal() +
 theme(
    legend.position = "none")

p2_worse_ai <- ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = payroll_growth,
    aes(x = year, y = payroll_growth_pct, group = naics_label),
    color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_line(
    data = payroll_growth_w4,
    aes(x = year, y = payroll_growth_pct, color = naics_label),
    linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(
    data = payroll_growth_w4,
    aes(x = year, y = payroll_growth_pct, color = naics_label),
    size = 1) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = payroll_growth_w4 |> filter(year == max(year)),
    aes(
      x = year,
      y = payroll_growth_pct,
      label = naics_label,
      color = naics_label),
    nudge_x = 1,
    show.legend = FALSE,
    size = 3) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "H") +
  labs(
    title = "Lower AI-Adoption Industries",
    subtitle = "Indexed to 2017 total payroll",
    x = "Year",
    y = NULL,
    caption = "Source: 2018 ABS + CS Panel Data") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

## final plot combining both plots above 
final_dis <- p1_top4_ai + p2_worse_ai +
  plot_layout(ncol = 2) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Payroll Growth Comparison",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14)))

final_dis

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Revenue
We only have revenue for the yeaer 2017, so I also looked at the revenue for that year by industry

Coderevenue_2017 <- cs_panel_clean |>
  filter(year == 2017) |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  summarise(total_revenue = sum(revenue * 1000, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(total_revenue > 0) |>
  arrange(desc(total_revenue))

#  2017 snapshot (in Trillions)
ggplot(revenue_2017, aes(x = total_revenue, y = reorder(naics_label, total_revenue))) +
  geom_col(fill = "grey30", width = 0.9) + 
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_dollar(scale = 1e-12, suffix = "T", accuracy = 1),
    breaks = seq(0, 10e12, by = 1e12),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05))) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Industry Revenue in 2017",
    subtitle = "A snapshot of financial volume right before the 2018 AI Survey",
    x = "Total Revenue (Trillions)",
    y = NULL,
    caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS) | Author: David Rios ") +  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + 
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: David’s Exploration
text:
The AI Wage Premium
Does the AI wage premium holds constant as firms grow in size?

Code## wage premium story
tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
         used_tech != "Unknown",
         naics_sector != "Total", 
         payroll > 0,
         n_emp > 0) |>
  mutate(
    ai_used = if_else(used_tech == "Used", "AI Adopters", "Non-Adopters"),
    payroll_per_emp = payroll / n_emp) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = n_emp, y = payroll_per_emp, color = ai_used)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, linewidth = 1.5) +  # method = "mgcv",
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::label_dollar(suffix = "K"), 
    breaks = seq(0, 300, by = 50)) +  
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  labs(
    title = "Does AI Pay?",
    subtitle = "Evidence of higher payroll standards among technology adopters across firm scales",
    x = "Number of Employees (Log Scale)",
    y = "Avg. Payroll per Employee (USD)",
    color = "Adoption Status",
    caption = "Payroll standardized to thousands of dollars per employee. Trend lines calculated via local regression (LOESS). \n Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS) | Author: David Rios"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(legend.position = "right",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 8))

title: David’s Exploration
text:
AI Hubs in the US
Geographically, where is AI growing the most rapidly? Or where is it AI adoption rate higher?

Code# AI Adoption Rate by State
state_ai_adoption <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    !is.na(used_tech),
    used_tech != "Unknown") |>
  group_by(state) |>
  summarise(
    total_firms = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    ai_firms = sum(n_firms[used_tech == "Used"], na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    state = str_pad(state, 2, pad = "0"),
    ai_rate = if_else(total_firms > 0, ai_firms / total_firms, NA_real_)) |>
  filter(total_firms > 0)

# Fetch map boundaries and shift AK/HI to the bottom
us_map <- tigris::states(cb = TRUE, resolution = "20m", class = "sf") |>
  filter(STUSPS != "PR") |>
  tigris::shift_geometry() |>
  mutate(GEOID = str_pad(GEOID, 2, pad = "0"))


  |                                                                            
  |                                                                      |   0%
  |                                                                            
  |==============================                                        |  43%
  |                                                                            
  |==================================                                    |  48%
  |                                                                            
  |======================================                                |  55%
  |                                                                            
  |====================================================                  |  74%
  |                                                                            
  |=======================================================               |  78%
  |                                                                            
  |===============================================================       |  89%
  |                                                                            
  |======================================================================| 100%

Codemap_data <- us_map |>
  left_join(state_ai_adoption, by = c("GEOID" = "state"))


ggplot(map_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = ai_rate), color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_distiller(
    palette = "OrRd",
    direction = 1,
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    na.value = "grey95") +
  labs(
    title = "Where are the AI Hubs?",
    subtitle = "AI Adoption Rate by State in 2018",
    fill = "Adoption\nRate",
    caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS)") +
  theme_void(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
    legend.position = "right")

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Wage Gaps and Premium
But is it worth to pay the cost? Are the revenues generated from AI higher than the cost of it?

Codetech_2018_summary <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    !is.na(used_tech),
    MOTUSETECH_LABEL == "All firms",
    IMPACTWF_U_LABEL == "All firms"    ) |>
  group_by(year, tech_type, used_tech, use_level) |>
  summarise(
    total_firms   = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_emp     = sum(n_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_payroll = sum(payroll, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    emp_per_firm = if_else(total_firms > 0, total_emp / total_firms, NA_real_),
    avg_wage     = if_else(total_emp > 0, total_payroll / total_emp, NA_real_))

industry_tech_summary <- tech_2018_clean |> 
  filter(
    !is.na(used_tech), 
    MOTUSETECH_LABEL == "All firms",   
    IMPACTWF_U_LABEL == "All firms"   ) |> 
  group_by(year, naics_label, tech_type, used_tech, use_level) |> 
  summarise(
    total_firms   = sum(n_firms, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_emp     = sum(n_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_payroll = sum(payroll, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups       = "drop") |> 
  mutate(
    emp_per_firm = if_else(total_firms > 0, total_emp / total_firms, NA_real_),
    avg_wage     = if_else(total_emp > 0, total_payroll / total_emp, NA_real_))

ai_industry_wages <- industry_tech_summary |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    used_tech %in% c("Did not use", "Used")) |>
  group_by(naics_label, used_tech) |>
  summarise(
    total_emp = sum(total_emp, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_payroll = sum(total_payroll, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(avg_wage = if_else(total_emp > 0, total_payroll / total_emp, NA_real_)) |>
  filter(total_emp > 0) 

# one row per industry with both groups side-by-side
wage_wide <- ai_industry_wages |>
  filter(used_tech %in% c("Did not use", "Used")) |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  filter(n() == 2) |>   # only industries where BOTH groups exist
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols    = naics_label,
    names_from = used_tech,
    values_from = c(avg_wage, total_emp, total_payroll)) |>
  rename(
    wage_no_ai = `avg_wage_Did not use`,
    wage_ai    = `avg_wage_Used`,
    emp_no_ai  = `total_emp_Did not use`,
    emp_ai     = `total_emp_Used`) 

 wage_wide <- wage_wide |>
  mutate(
    wage_gap = wage_ai - wage_no_ai,
    premium_pct = if_else(wage_no_ai > 0, wage_gap / wage_no_ai, NA_real_),
    total_emp = emp_no_ai + emp_ai,
    ai_emp_share = if_else(total_emp > 0, emp_ai / total_emp, NA_real_))



Code##  The AI Wage Gap by Industry
dumbbell_long <- wage_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(wage_no_ai, wage_ai),
    names_to = "group",
    values_to = "avg_wage") |>
  mutate(
    group = if_else(group == "wage_ai", "AI Adopters", "Non-Adopters"),
    naics_label = reorder(naics_label, wage_gap))

ggplot() +
  geom_segment(
    data = wage_wide |> mutate(naics_label = reorder(naics_label, wage_gap)),
    aes(x = wage_no_ai, xend = wage_ai,
        y = naics_label, yend = naics_label),
    color = "grey75", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(
    data = dumbbell_long,
    aes(x = avg_wage, y = naics_label, color = group),
    size = 4) +
  scale_color_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::label_dollar(suffix = "K")) +
  labs(
    title = "The AI Wage Gap by Industry",
    subtitle = "AI adopters vs. non-adopters (2018)",
    x = "Avg. Annual Payroll per Employee (Thousands USD)",
    y = NULL,
    color = NULL,
    caption = "Source: 2018 ABS") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 8))






Code## AI Wage Premium: Who Benefits Most?

wage_wide |>
  mutate(
    naics_label  = reorder(naics_label, premium_pct),
    premium_sign = if_else(premium_pct >= 0, "Positive", "Negative")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = premium_pct, y = naics_label, fill = premium_sign)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.75) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "grey40", linewidth = 0.5) +
  # Label the bars with the % value
  geom_text(
    aes(
      label = scales::percent(premium_pct, accuracy = 1),
      hjust = if_else(premium_pct >= 0, -0.15, 1.15)
    ),
    size = 3, color = "grey20"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.08, 0.10))
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Positive" = "#2d6a4f", "Negative" = "#c1121f")) +
  labs(
    title    = "AI Wage Premium: Who Benefits Most?",
    subtitle = "% difference in avg. pay between AI and non-AI firms, by industry (2018)",
    x        = "Wage Premium (%)",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS)\nNote: Food Services and Information sectors show the largest wage premiums for AI-adopting firms."
  )






Code## Employment Scale vs. Wage Premium
ggplot(
  wage_wide |> mutate(premium_sign = if_else(premium_pct >= 0, "Positive", "Negative")),
  aes(
    x = ai_emp_share,
    y = premium_pct,
    size = total_emp,
    color = premium_sign,
    label = naics_label)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.15, linetype = "dotted", color = "grey60", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.65) +
  geom_text(
    size = 3,
    color = "grey20") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.05)) ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::percent_format(accuracy = 1),
    limits = c(-0.15, 0.75) ) +
  scale_size_continuous(
    range = c(3, 16),
    labels = scales::label_number(scale = 1e-6, suffix = "M", accuracy = 1),
    name = "Total Employees" ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Positive" = "#2d6a4f", "Negative" = "#c1121f"),
    guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title = "Where Does AI Create the Most Value?",
    subtitle = "Share of industry workforce at AI firms vs. wage premium",
    x = "Share of Industry Employees at AI Firms",
    y = "AI Wage Premium",
    caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey (ABS)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey30"),
    plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, color = "grey40", size = 8) )

title: David’s Exploration
text: Patterns within Industries

title: David’s Exploration
text:
AI Adoption Spatial

Note: industry sectors with more than 10 missing states were excluded from this analysis. These sectors are Agriculture, Mining, and Utilities. The remaining sectors provide a more reliable look at AI adoption trends. AI adoption rates are rendered to two decimal places to capture variations in sectors where adoption is emerging but still below 1%.

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Takeaways

AI adoption in 2018 was driven by firm scale, not industry wealth or geography.


Adoption did not follow the money

Management led AI adoption at 7.7% (Figure 1), yet ranked near the bottom in total 2017 revenue (Figure 4). Wholesale Trade and Manufacturing — the two largest industries by revenue —were mid-tier adopters at best. Knowledge-intensive sectors moved first because AI fits their work, not because they had the deepest pockets.

The wage premium is real — and largest where adoption is rarest

AI adopters paid more at every firm size (Figure 5). But the premium was widest in sectors where adoption was still uncommon: Food Services led at 86%, followed by Health Care (68%) and Wholesale Trade (67%) — all low-to-mid adoption industries (Figure 6). When most peers adopt, the competitive advantage narrows; Finance and Insurance, a top adopter, showed only 24%. The bubble chart (Figure 7) confirms this: industries with the highest premiums tend to have a smaller share of workers actually at AI firms, meaning early movers are still rare enough to capture outsized returns.

Geography is a proxy for industrial composition

California dominates the adoption map (Figure 8), but the random forest assigns State near-zero permutation importance once firm characteristics are known (Figure 10). The geographic pattern reflects where Management, Information, and Professional Services firms concentrate — not something intrinsic to location.

Payroll growth favoured AI-adopting industries — with a COVID caveat

The top four AI adopters grew payroll steadily from 2017 to 2021 (Figure 2). Lower-adoption sectors, particularly Agriculture and Arts & Recreation, showed sharp swings around 2019–2020 (Figure 3) — a COVID artifact driven by in-person service disruption, not a structural signal. AI-adopting industries’ relative stability over the same period may partly reflect the resilience of remote-compatible, technology-augmented operations.

The forest confirmed scale as the dominant factor

The single decision tree (Figure 10) splits first on number of firms ≥ 558, then on employees per firm ≥ 61 — a profile that maps directly onto the concentrated, knowledge-intensive industries at the top of Figure 1. Adding 2017 panel variables — revenue per firm and average industry wage — cut OOB prediction error from 5.3% to 3.1%, the largest accuracy gain in the analysis. Pre-adoption financial context carries genuine signal that cross-sectional 2018 data alone cannot capture.
The Gini vs. permutation comparison (Figure 9) shows that Gini inflated the apparent importance of continuous size variables. Permutation importance redistributed credit more evenly and elevated employees-per-firm — suggesting AI adoption reflects a single latent dimension of firm scale rather than any one metric in isolation.
This analysis is observational. Whether AI adoption caused the subsequent payroll divergence, or whether the same organizational characteristics that predict adoption also predict payroll growth, cannot be resolved with this data — and remains the natural next step.

title: David’s Exploration
text:
Appendix
Machine Learning to predict AI Usage
After doing my EDA in the previous document, I was interested in whether we can predict which firms adopt AI. Thus, I decided to run a Random Forest.
Random Forest
The random forest algorithm extends bagging by combining both bagging and feature randomness to create an uncorrelated forest of decision trees. Decision trees work by asking a series of questions that split the data into branches. They are trained using the Classification and Regression Tree (CART) algorithm, with metrics like Gini impurity, information gain, or MSE (mean std. errors) used to evaluate split quality.
Building the Forest
While initial results using Gini-based importance suggested that firm size variables overwhelmingly drive AI adoption, this measure is known to be biased toward continuous predictors. Using permutation importance — which evaluates the impact of each variable on model accuracy — provides a more reliable ranking. The results confirm that firm scale remains the primary predictor of AI adoption, followed by workforce intensity and industry sector. In contrast, geographic location (state) contributes no predictive power once firm characteristics are considered.
Because firm size variables are highly correlated, permutation importance likely distributes predictive contribution across them, suggesting that AI adoption is driven by an underlying latent factor of firm scale rather than any single metric.

Codetidymodels_prefer()

# 2017 industry financials (pre-adoption context)
industry_financials <- cs_panel_clean |>
  filter(year == 2017) |>
  group_by(naics_label) |>
  summarise(
    revenue_per_firm = mean(revenue_per_firm, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_wage_panel   = mean(avg_wage,         na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop")

#  9-predictor dataset
rf_data_9 <- tech_2018_clean |>
  filter(
    tech_type == "artificial intelligence",
    used_tech %in% c("Used", "Did not use"),
    !is.na(n_emp), !is.na(payroll), !is.na(n_firms),
    n_emp > 0, payroll > 0, n_firms > 0,
    naics_sector != "Total" ) |>
  mutate(
    ai_adopted   = as.factor(if_else(used_tech == "Used", "Yes", "No")),
    avg_wage     = payroll / n_emp,
    emp_per_firm = n_emp / n_firms,
    naics_label  = as.factor(naics_label),
    state        = as.factor(state) ) |>
  left_join(industry_financials, by = "naics_label") |>
  select(
    ai_adopted,
    # 7-predictor core
    n_emp, n_firms, avg_wage, emp_per_firm, payroll, naics_label, state,
    # +2 from panel
    revenue_per_firm, avg_wage_panel ) |>
  drop_na()

# 7-predictor subset
rf_data_7 <- rf_data_9 |>
  select(-revenue_per_firm, -avg_wage_panel)

cat("Rows in both datasets:", nrow(rf_data_7), "(same rows, different columns)\n")

Rows in both datasets: 1489 (same rows, different columns)

Codecat("7-predictor columns:", ncol(rf_data_7) - 1, "\n")

7-predictor columns: 7 

Codecat("9-predictor columns:", ncol(rf_data_9) - 1, "\n")

9-predictor columns: 9 

Code#Shared label map
var_labels <- c(
  "n_emp"            = "# Employees",
  "n_firms"          = "# Firms",
  "avg_wage"         = "Avg Wage (2018)",
  "emp_per_firm"     = "Employees / Firm",
  "payroll"          = "Total Payroll",
  "naics_label"      = "Industry Sector",
  "state"            = "State",
  "revenue_per_firm" = "Revenue / Firm (2017)",
  "avg_wage_panel"   = "Avg Industry Wage (2017)")



Code# settings: 1000 trees, min_n = 2, sqrt(p) predictors tried per split -- AKA random forest
make_spec <- function(importance_type) {
  rand_forest() |>
    set_mode("classification") |>
    set_engine("ranger") |>
    set_args(
      mtry        = NULL,       
      trees       = 1000,
      min_n       = 2,
      probability = FALSE,
      importance  = importance_type)
}

spec_gini  <- make_spec("impurity")    # Gini / mean decrease in impurity
spec_perm  <- make_spec("permutation") # Permutation importance


# Fit all four forests
set.seed(212)
forest_7_gini <- spec_gini |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data_7)
forest_7_perm <- spec_perm |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data_7)

set.seed(212)
forest_9_gini <- spec_gini |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data_9)
forest_9_perm <- spec_perm |> fit(ai_adopted ~ ., data = rf_data_9)


#  OOB error comparison table
oob_summary <- tibble(
  Model      = c("7 predictors", "7 predictors", "9 predictors", "9 predictors"),
  Importance = c("Gini", "Permutation", "Gini", "Permutation"),
  OOB_Error  = c(
    forest_7_gini |> extract_fit_engine() |> pluck("prediction.error"),
    forest_7_perm |> extract_fit_engine() |> pluck("prediction.error"),
    forest_9_gini |> extract_fit_engine() |> pluck("prediction.error"),
    forest_9_perm |> extract_fit_engine() |> pluck("prediction.error"))) |>
  mutate(OOB_Accuracy = 1 - OOB_Error)

print(oob_summary)

# A tibble: 4 × 4
  Model        Importance  OOB_Error OOB_Accuracy
  <chr>        <chr>           <dbl>        <dbl>
1 7 predictors Gini           0.0557        0.944
2 7 predictors Permutation    0.0517        0.948
3 9 predictors Gini           0.0302        0.970
4 9 predictors Permutation    0.0302        0.970



Code# Visual: OOB error side-by-side
oob_summary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Model, y = OOB_Error, fill = Importance)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.55) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = scales::percent(OOB_Error, accuracy = 0.1)),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.55),
    vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Gini" = "#2d6a4f", "Permutation" = "#f4a582")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    title    = "OOB Prediction Error: 7 vs 9 Predictors",
    subtitle = "Lower is better — error rate on out-of-bag samples",
    x        = NULL, y = "OOB Error Rate", fill = "Importance method",
    caption  = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey + 2017 Economic Census"
  )






Code# Importance extraction helper
get_importance <- function(forest, method_label, n_pred_label) {
  forest |>
    extract_fit_engine() |>
    pluck("variable.importance") |>
    enframe(name = "Variable", value = "Importance") |>
    mutate(
      Variable   = recode(Variable, !!!var_labels),
      Method     = method_label,
      Predictors = n_pred_label,
      # Normalise within each forest so Gini and Permutation scales are comparable
      Importance = Importance / max(abs(Importance))
    )
}

importance_all <- bind_rows(
  get_importance(forest_7_gini, "Gini",        "7 predictors"),
  get_importance(forest_7_perm, "Permutation",  "7 predictors"),
  get_importance(forest_9_gini, "Gini",        "9 predictors"),
  get_importance(forest_9_perm, "Permutation",  "9 predictors")
)


#  Importance comparison: Gini vs Permutation (within same predictor set)
plot_importance_comparison <- function(data, title_suffix) {
  data |>
    select(Variable, Method, Importance) |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = Method, values_from = Importance) |>
    mutate(Variable = fct_reorder(Variable, Gini)) |>
    ggplot(aes(y = Variable)) +
    geom_segment(
      aes(x = Permutation, xend = Gini, yend = Variable),
      color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.6
    ) +
    geom_point(aes(x = Gini,        color = "Gini"),        size = 3) +
    geom_point(aes(x = Permutation, color = "Permutation"), size = 3) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Gini" = "#2d6a4f", "Permutation" = "#e76f51")) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    labs(
      title    = glue::glue("Gini vs Permutation Importance — {title_suffix}"),
      subtitle = "Normalised 0–1 within each method. Dots far apart = Gini bias.",
      x = "Normalised importance", y = NULL, color = NULL,
      caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey"
    ) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
}

p7 <- importance_all |>
  filter(Predictors == "7 predictors") |>
  plot_importance_comparison("7 predictors")

p9 <- importance_all |>
  filter(Predictors == "9 predictors") |>
  plot_importance_comparison("9 predictors")

p7 / p9   # patchwork: stacked






Code#  Faceted importance
importance_all |>
  group_by(Method, Predictors) |>
  mutate(Variable = fct_reorder(Variable, Importance)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Importance, y = Variable, color = Method)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Gini" = "#2d6a4f", "Permutation" = "#e76f51")) +
  facet_grid(Predictors ~ Method, scales = "free_y") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Variable Importance: All Four Models",
    subtitle = "Normalised importance — rows sorted independently per panel",
    x = "Normalised importance", y = NULL, color = NULL,
    caption = "Source: 2018 Annual Business Survey + 2017 Economic Census"
  )






Code# OOB Confusion matrices
# (using the permutation forests since they have more honest importance estimates)
cat("\n── Confusion matrix: 7-predictor forest (permutation) ──\n")


── Confusion matrix: 7-predictor forest (permutation) ──

Codeforest_7_perm |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  pluck("confusion.matrix") |>
  t() |>
  print()

         true
predicted  No Yes
      No  812  32
      Yes  45 600

Codecat("\n── Confusion matrix: 9-predictor forest (permutation) ──\n")


── Confusion matrix: 9-predictor forest (permutation) ──

Codeforest_9_perm |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  pluck("confusion.matrix") |>
  t() |>
  print()

         true
predicted  No Yes
      No  836  24
      Yes  21 608

Code# Single decision tree 
# The forest uses 1000 of these, each on a bootstrap sample with random feature
# subsets — individual trees are weak but their average is robust.

single_tree <- decision_tree() |>
  set_mode("classification") |>
  set_engine("rpart") |>
  set_args(cost_complexity = 0.02, tree_depth = 4) |>
  fit(
    ai_adopted ~ n_firms + emp_per_firm + avg_wage + payroll + n_emp,
    data = rf_data_7   # no state & naics_label to keep the tree readable
  )

single_tree |>
  extract_fit_engine() |>
  rpart.plot(
    type        = 4,
    extra       = 104,          # show % and n at each leaf
    box.palette = "GnRd",
    roundint    = FALSE,
    fallen.leaves = TRUE,      
    main = paste(
      "One example tree (depth 4, cost_complexity = 0.02)",
      "The random forest grows 1000 of these on bootstrapped samples",
      "Each split considers only sqrt(5) ≈ 2 randomly chosen predictors",
      sep = "\n"
    )
  )

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text: Title: AI Usage For US Companies

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Short Description of the Project:
With AI usage soaring in recent years, our way of life has drastically changed. For this project, we will observe how this surge has impacted businesses across the US.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
2-3 General Research Questions:
1.  How has company AI usage changed over time?

2.  How does company AI usage vary by location, such as state or
    urban/rural status?

3.  How might company AI usage vary by internal factors, such as
    company size and ownership?

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Reasons/Inspirations Behind this Project:
Our group is composed of Economics majors, so we want to do a project that is related to our field. Additionally, we want to focus on recently developing news in order to make our project as relevant as possible, which is how we landed on AI usage as our topic.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
One Potential Dataset:
Annual Business Survey (ABS) Data 2017-2023: https://www.census.gov/data/developers/data-sets/abs.html

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Communication Plan:
We will communicate primarily through GChat, and each team member will respond as soon as possible, with more leeway given during holidays and weekends. We will set up meetings outside of class on a needs-based basis, with all members having allocated 11am-12pm on Fridays for such meetings. More meetings may be scheduled depending on the workload of a milestone.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Roles:

Primary Point of Contact: Katherine Manuel
Technical Lead: David Torres
Style Lead: Isauro Reyes

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Conflict Resolution SOP:
If a team member misses a deadline, the other team members will reach out to them and determine how to proceed (i.e. if they need extra help, if we as a team need to request an extension, etc.). The matter will only be elevated to the professor if absolutely necessary, which we do not anticipate. For evenly split decisions where the team doesn’t know how to proceed, we will flip a coin/decide randomly. However, we don’t expect this to be an issue since there are 3 of us.

title: Appendix A — Proposal
text:
Appendix
Note: we moved our data import and cleaning to new R files, for the convenience!

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Introduction
Source: THE DIFFERENCES IN HOW CNN, MSNBC & FOX COVER THE NEWS pudding.coo
In this document, we will be talking about the data story structure of the article “The differences in how CNN, MSNBC & Fox Cover News.” This is accessed through The Pudding’s website, which we believe is a competition where people can narrate with the data. The author of this article is Charlie Smart.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What Did They Do?

Clustering technique, but simple visualizations.

Visualization animations based on user scrolling bring the reader’s attention to different, important aspects of the visualizations and different subsets in the data. Readers can hover the cursor over individual points.

Building the visualization with the reader.

Each visualization starts off bare. With each paragraph of text to the side of the viz, the author adds more data to the visualization, allowing text and viz to complement each other.   

Search bars & filtering options for specific visualizations. 

After the visualizations have been explored fully, the author gives the user a search bar, in which the user can input their own topics/key words and see how they’re reflected in each plot.
Audiences can also filter data points/subsets of the data, allowing them to focus on aspects of the dataset that they are interested in. 

Interactive text. 

Certain words (ex. Month names, definitions) in the text allow audiences to hover their cursor over it, which either results in the respective portion of a graph being highlighted or visualizations of the definition being described to pop up on screen. This allows audiences to achieve a more complete understanding of what the author is talking about.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Implications & Methodology

What’s the purpose of these plots?

Following the visualizations, the author makes light of the scope of political polarization, warning the user to be aware of the news they are receiving and the agenda they may be pushing. This caution makes the visualizations even stronger, as they highlight this warning in an extremely effective way.

What was the author’s creation process?

At the end of the article, the author adds a section on Data and Methodology, allowing audiences better insight into data sources/what choices went into data collection, and other underlying data aspects that would otherwise be hidden in the visualizations.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
CONCLUSIONS

The audience can draw their own conclusions based on which words are used most by the big 3 news outlets: CNN, MSNBC, and FOX. This is informative as we can see which ones are biased to certain positions or ideas. This is precisely what the authors of this project try to do by selecting keywords the audience might be interested in or events happening during that time, such as the Russian war, hurricanes, Trump, Hillary, etc. 
Most importantly, we gathered more ideas on the possible direction our project can take. As our project is on the AI implementation in U.S. businesses, we probably won’t do it based on words, but rather in companies, highlighting the outliers but also those left behind. What industries are not catching up in the AI implementation? What companies are close to the 100% AI implementation? Is it possible to get 100% implementation?
